event: The Lucayan people, an Arawakan-speaking branch of the Taino, begin migrating from Hispaniola and Cuba to settle the islands of the Bahamas.
Lucayan settlement expands across the archipelago, with communities supported by fishing, conch harvesting, and the cultivation of cassava.
Christopher Columbus makes his first New World landfall on a Bahamian island the Lucayans called Guanahani, which he named San Salvador, initiating European contact.
Juan Ponce de Leon passes through the Bahamas during his search for Bimini and the fabled fountain of youth.
Spanish slave raids deport the Lucayan population to work in mines and pearl fisheries, effectively depopulating the islands within a few decades.
English Puritan settlers known as the Eleutheran Adventurers, led by William Sayle, establish a colony on Eleuthera seeking religious freedom.
King Charles II grants the Bahamas to the Lords Proprietors of Carolina, formalizing English claims to the islands.
Spanish forces from Cuba attack and destroy the English settlement at Charles Town on New Providence in retaliation for piracy.
The settlement of Charles Town is renamed Nassau in honor of King William III of the House of Orange-Nassau.
Pirates effectively control Nassau, turning New Providence into a notorious base during the golden age of piracy.
Woodes Rogers arrives as the first royal governor and suppresses piracy under the motto 'Expulsis Piratis, Restituta Commercia.'
The first Bahamian colonial assembly meets in Nassau, establishing representative government in the islands.
American Continental Navy forces briefly seize Nassau during the American Revolutionary War to capture military stores.
Spanish forces capture Nassau and occupy the Bahamas near the end of the American Revolutionary War.
Loyalist Andrew Deveaux recaptures Nassau, and the Treaty of Paris formally restores the Bahamas to British control.
American Loyalists fleeing the new United States arrive with enslaved people, expanding cotton plantation agriculture across the islands.
Britain abolishes the transatlantic slave trade, after which the Royal Navy resettles liberated Africans in the Bahamas.
Slavery is abolished throughout the British Empire, emancipating enslaved people in the Bahamas under an apprenticeship system.
Full emancipation takes effect as the apprenticeship system ends, freeing the formerly enslaved Bahamian population.
Nassau becomes a major blockade-running hub supplying the Confederacy during the American Civil War, bringing a brief economic boom.
A devastating hurricane strikes the islands, causing severe damage as the Civil War economic boom collapses.
Henry Flagler opens the Hotel Colonial in Nassau, launching the Bahamas as a winter tourist destination for wealthy Americans.
American Prohibition turns the Bahamas into a bootlegging center, fueling a liquor-smuggling boom centered on Nassau and Bimini.
Pan American Airways begins air service to Nassau, opening regular air links between the Bahamas and the United States.
The Duke of Windsor, the former King Edward VIII, becomes Governor of the Bahamas during the Second World War.
The Burma Road riot erupts in Nassau as Black laborers protest unequal wages on wartime airfield construction projects.
The Progressive Liberal Party is founded, mobilizing the Black majority against the white minority Bay Street merchant elite.
A general strike led by labor leader Randol Fawkes paralyzes Nassau and accelerates demands for political and economic reform.
Women win the right to vote in the Bahamas, a major milestone achieved through the suffrage movement.
The Bahamas gains internal self-government under a new constitution while remaining a British colony.
The Progressive Liberal Party under Lynden Pindling wins power, marking the first Black majority government in Bahamian history.
The colony's name is formally changed to the Commonwealth of the Bahamas, reflecting expanded self-rule.
The Bahamas achieves full independence from the United Kingdom on July 10 with Lynden Pindling as the first prime minister.
The Bahamas is admitted to the United Nations as a sovereign member state.
The Bahamas joins the Organization of American States, deepening its regional diplomatic engagement.
An international drug-trafficking scandal implicates senior government figures, tarnishing the Pindling administration.
Hubert Ingraham and the Free National Movement defeat the PLP, ending Lynden Pindling's 25 years in power.
Hurricane Floyd causes extensive damage across the northern Bahamas, prompting major relief and rebuilding efforts.
Perry Christie leads the PLP back to power, winning the general election after a decade in opposition.
Bahamian sprinters and the men's relay team win Olympic medals in London, boosting the nation's athletic prestige.
The cargo ship El Faro sinks near the Bahamas during Hurricane Joaquin, killing all 33 crew in a major maritime disaster.
Hubert Minnis and the Free National Movement win a landslide election victory over Perry Christie's PLP.
Hurricane Dorian, a catastrophic Category 5 storm, devastates Abaco and Grand Bahama, killing dozens and displacing thousands.
The Bahamas launches the Sand Dollar, becoming one of the first countries to issue a nationwide central bank digital currency.
Philip Davis leads the PLP to victory in a snap election held amid the COVID-19 pandemic, becoming prime minister.
